quotes.verbatim_quote:
A leading tone
  is what we call the note that resolves (or leads to) another tone a half step above or a half step
  below it. However, functionally speaking it is the VII degree of the diatonic scale (Natural
  Major Scale). Therefore, the note "B" is the leading tone of the "C" note which gives us the
  feeling of tension and preparation for this tone.
quotes.why_load_bearing: The whole chapter's framework rests on this functional definition of leading tone as the VII degree producing tension-and-resolution.

quotes.topic: Origin: Natural Minor had no leading tone
quotes.verbatim_quote:
One of the greatest musical problems for the church
  choir at the time was to be able to sing the Natural Minor Scale in tune and this happened for
  one reason: the Natural Minor Scale had no leading key.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Historical premise that motivates the entire derivation of harmonic/melodic minor scales.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
So, to end this problem, the VII degree of the Natural Minor Scale was changed half a step up,
  forming a scale as complete as the Natural Major Scale because now it has a Leading Tone. That
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the construction rule that produces the Harmonic Minor Scale from the Natural Minor.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
In addition to the Harmonic Minor Scale, another scale was created: the Melodic Minor Scale.
  Initially, that scale was used only in its ascending order, because the descending order was
  difficult to sing in tune, as it sounded a lot like the Natural Major Scale, and contained a minor
  3rd at the end.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Explains the historical asymmetric (ascending-only) origin of melodic minor.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Currently the Melodic Minor Scale is considered by most modern authors as being equal to the
Bach Scale, meaning that it has only one form, both ascending and descending.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Establishes the book's working definition of melodic minor as a single symmetric scale.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
In the mid-twentieth century, the Russian composer Nicolai A. Rimsky-Korsakov, named
another variation of the Natural Major Scale: Harmonic Major Scale
quotes.why_load_bearing: Attributes and names the third new scale system the chapter introduces.

quotes.topic: Three new scale families generate modes
quotes.verbatim_quote:
Just like the natural major and natural minor scale, each one of these three new scales generate
seven different modes, creating three groups of seven scales each. In theory, we could also
consider them harmonic fields, however in order to avoid confusion with names, we will call
them"scale modes".
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the organizing principle of the chapter: three families x seven modes, called scale modes not harmonic fields.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
It is important to understant that we cannot use the chord families derived from these three
groups of scale modes to guide us through songs, we can only use them to add more "colors" to
the chords.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Critical rule: these derived chord families are color/tension devices, not structural progressions.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Im Harmonic Minor: Root /maj 2nd /min 3rd /perf 4th /perf 5th /min 6th /maj 7th
quotes.why_load_bearing: Canonical interval spelling for the Harmonic Minor Im mode (the parent scale).

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Avoid notes: minor 6th when the chord is Am(maj7)
Characteristic notes: major 7th
quotes.why_load_bearing: Pattern example of how each derived mode is specified by avoid + characteristic notes.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
VERY IMPORTANT:

      Not all extended notes of a chord must be in it.

      Many of them should only be played as the passing tones of a melody on the chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Stated as VERY IMPORTANT across all three scale families - extensions inform melody, not necessarily voicings.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The best way to not get lost on the guitar fretboard is to /irst visualize the bass note of the
chord. However, different from scales, we must visualize the root of the chord (bass note)
only using the 5th and 6th strings.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Core fretboard visualization rule used to navigate each scale's chord family.

quotes.verbatim_quote: Im Melodic Minor: Root /maj 2nd /min 3rd /perf 4th /perf 5th /maj 6th /maj 7th
quotes.why_load_bearing: Canonical interval spelling for the Melodic Minor Im mode (the parent scale).

quotes.verbatim_quote: VII Altered : Root /min 2nd /aug 2nd /maj 3rd /aug 4th /min 6th /min 7th
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the Altered Scale (G7alt) as the VIIth mode of Melodic Minor - load-bearing for jazz dominant theory.

quotes.verbatim_quote: bIII Lydian Augmented: Root /maj 2nd /maj 3rd /aug 4th /aug 5th /maj 6th /maj 7th
quotes.why_load_bearing: Canonical spelling of Lydian Augmented as bIII of Melodic Minor - signature jazz major7#5 source.

quotes.verbatim_quote: IV Lydian Dominant: Root /maj 2nd /maj 3rd /aug 4th /perf 5th /maj 6th /min 7th
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines Lydian Dominant (7#11) as the IV of Melodic Minor - core jazz dominant device.

quotes.verbatim_quote: I Harmonic Major: Root /maj 2nd /maj 3rd /perf 4th /perf 5th /min 6th /maj 7th
quotes.why_load_bearing: Canonical interval spelling for the Harmonic Major parent scale - major scale with b6.

quotes.verbatim_quote: bVI Lydian Augmented (#2/ #5): Root /aug 2nd /maj 3rd /aug 4th /aug 5th /maj 6th /maj 7th
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the doubly-augmented mode that emerges from Harmonic Major bVI - distinct from the Melodic Minor Lydian Augmented.

quotes.verbatim_quote: VIIm Locrian (bb7): Root /min 2nd /min 3rd /perf 4th /dim 5th /min 6th /dim 7th
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the fully diminished 7th mode of Harmonic Major - source for o7(b13) and (11) chord colors.

quotes.verbatim_quote: a) Play the harmonic /ields in the same key until you feel comfortable.
quotes.why_load_bearing: First and primary practice rule applied identically to all three scale families.

quotes.verbatim_quote: b) Always study with rhythm (funk, swing, etc). It helps you to memorize things faster.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Pedagogical rule: rhythmic context aids memorization of the chord families.

quotes.topic: Study device: chord proximity for 6th/5th strings
quotes.verbatim_quote:
d) To make visualization easier, choose chords that are close to each other, using only the
   6th and 5th string chords. The 4th string chords should be used as soon as you get
   confortable with the others.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Method rule constraining how the learner approaches each new scale family on the fretboard.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
The best way to not get lost on the guitar fretboard, is to /irst visualize the bass note of the
chord. However, different from scales, we must visualize the root of the chord (bass note)
only using the 5th and 6th strings.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Restated for Melodic Minor (with comma variant) showing the visualization rule is universal across the three families.
